strands.title: Replay Module Architecture & Requirements
strands.content: A new offline simulation module is being designed at src/pichay/replay.py to measure the effectiveness of context paging by replaying historical interactions. The module must parse proxy JSONL logs, reconstruct message arrays as they existed at each API call, apply pager.py compaction logic to snapshots, detect page faults (cases where evicted content was re-requested), and report metrics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). The module exposes a CLI interface via argparse accepting log file paths, configurable thresholds (--age-threshold default 4, --min-size default 500), and --json for structured output. The conversation now enters the **PRE-IMPLEMENTATION READING PHASE**: the assistant has acknowledged the need to read pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py thoroughly before writing any code. This reading phase is critical because it will ground all subsequent design and implementation decisions in the actual codebase structure, not assumptions.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs and reconstruct message arrays at each API call turn
Paging effectiveness is measurable through offline replay — comparing actual next actions against simulated evictions reveals page faults
Key metrics are fault rate, bytes saved, and reduction percentage, reported per-session and in aggregate
CLI interface requires --age-threshold (default 4), --min-size (default 500), --json flag, and accepts multiple log file paths
Pre-implementation code reading is mandatory to ground design in actual codebase structure

strands.title: Dependency Analysis & Pre-Implementation Requirements
strands.content: Three core dependencies must be understood before implementation: (1) pager.py contains the compaction logic that replay.py will invoke on reconstructed snapshots—the assistant must identify the main compaction function, its parameters, return value, and the precise mechanism/data representation of 'evicted' content; (2) proxy.py defines the JSONL log format and logging behavior—the assistant must extract the exact schema, field names, data types, nesting, and how sessions/API calls are represented; (3) eval.py may contain evaluation patterns, metrics computation utilities, and reporting conventions—the assistant should identify how metrics are aggregated, formatted, and output. The reading phase has been initiated as the next step in the workflow. After reading, the assistant will formulate a precise data flow diagram and identify concrete data structures (dict, dataclass, custom object) needed for snapshots, eviction events, and faults.

key_claims.text: pager.py contains the compaction logic that replay.py will invoke on reconstructed snapshots
proxy.py defines the JSONL log format that replay.py must parse
eval.py may provide evaluation patterns and metrics computation utilities
Reading these modules is necessary before writing any code to ground design in reality

strands.title: Core Simulation Logic: Snapshot Reconstruction & Fault Detection
strands.content: The replay simulation operates on a snapshot-by-snapshot basis within a pre-reading phase. For each turn in a session, the module will: (1) reconstruct the messages array as it existed before that API call, (2) apply pager compaction logic with configured thresholds, (3) check whether the model's actual next action references any content that was evicted. A match between evicted content and a subsequent request constitutes a page fault. This logic requires careful state tracking across turns within a session, proper understanding of what 'eviction' means in the pager's algorithm (to be determined by reading pager.py), and reliable extraction of the 'actual next action' from the log (to be determined by reading proxy.py). The simulation is deterministic and reproducible. **Current status**: Logic is conceptual and awaits grounding through code review.

key_claims.text: Snapshot reconstruction requires tracking message array state before each API call turn
A page fault occurs when the model requests content that was evicted by pager compaction in the prior turn
Pager compaction logic is applied within the simulation loop, not at logging time
Simulation is deterministic given log input and configuration parameters
The exact mechanism of fault detection awaits specification from reading pager.py and proxy.py

strands.title: Metrics & Reporting
strands.content: Two levels of metrics are required. Per-session metrics include fault count, fault rate (faults / total turns), total bytes saved, and reduction percentage (bytes saved / original size). Aggregate metrics roll these up across all sessions in the input. The --json flag triggers machine-readable output (JSON format) rather than human-readable text. The module must track both the state before and after compaction to compute bytes saved and reduction percentage accurately. **Current status**: The structure of per-session and aggregate metrics is clear, but the precise output format (both text and JSON) will be informed by reading eval.py to identify existing reporting patterns and conventions in the codebase.

key_claims.text: Per-session metrics: fault count, fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage
Aggregate metrics are computed across all sessions in the input
--json flag enables machine-readable output (JSON format)
Bytes saved is computable as (original message array size - compacted size) per turn
Output formatting patterns will be grounded by reading eval.py

strands.title: Pre-Implementation Reading Workflow
strands.content: The assistant has committed to a structured reading phase with clear priorities: (1) READ pager.py to identify the main compaction function, parameters, return value, data/mechanism of eviction, and how it tracks what was removed; (2) READ proxy.py to extract the JSONL schema, field names, data types, session identification, API call representation, and how 'next action' is encoded; (3) READ eval.py to understand metrics computation patterns, aggregation logic, formatting conventions, CLI handling, and output structure. After reading, the assistant will formulate: (a) a precise data flow diagram from log parsing to metrics reporting, (b) concrete data structures (dict, dataclass, or custom object) for snapshots, eviction events, and faults, (c) error handling strategy for malformed logs or edge cases, (d) CLI structure via argparse, (e) coding style checklist from existing modules. This reading phase gates implementation and ensures the subsequent design is empirically grounded, not assumption-based.

key_claims.text: Reading pager.py is the first priority to understand compaction mechanics
Reading proxy.py is the second priority to understand log structure and action encoding
Reading eval.py is the third priority to identify reporting patterns
Post-reading outputs will include data flow diagram, concrete data structures, and CLI design
The reading phase is mandatory before writing any implementation code

open_questions: What is the exact structure of the JSONL log format produced by proxy.py? (field names, data types, nesting)
How does pager.py define and implement compaction? What is the signature of the key function to call?
How is the model's 'next action' encoded in or extractable from the proxy log?
What constitutes an 'evicted' item in the context of the pager? Messages? Message subranges?
How should page faults be detected — exact string/ID matching, fuzzy matching, or semantic matching?
Should the replay module reconstruct a full message array in memory, or stream through the log?
Are there edge cases (e.g., first turn, session boundaries, malformed logs) that require special handling?
Should the per-session report be printed immediately after each session, or accumulated and printed at the end?
Does eval.py provide a template for metrics aggregation and reporting?
What Python version and dependencies (if any) are available for this module?
What is the return value structure of pager's compaction function—what does it reveal about what was evicted?
How does proxy.py track message history within a session—is it cumulative or per-turn?
What data format does eval.py use for JSON output (if any)?
